g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 3 on Regression Analysis, a cornerstone technique in machine learning for predicting continuous values.
g.text: Module 3: Regression Analysis in Machine Learning

g.data-narration: Regression predicts continuous numerical outcomes by modeling relationships between input variables and a target value.
g.text: A supervised learning technique that predicts continuous output values based on
input features. Unlike classification (discrete labels), regression estimates quantities.

g.data-narration: Regression differs from classification by predicting any numerical value along a continuous range, not just categories.
Simple Linear Regression models the relationship between one feature and one target using a straight line equation.
g.text: Models relationship between ONE independent variable (x) and ONE dependent variable (y)
using a straight line. The goal: find the best-fit line through the data points.

g.data-narration: The linear regression equation has five components: the target y, input x, intercept β₀, slope β₁, and error ε.
Mean Squared Error measures how well our line fits the data by averaging the squared differences between predictions and actual values.
g.text: The MSE quantifies the error between predicted values and actual values.

g.data-narration: Gradient Descent is an optimization algorithm that iteratively adjusts parameters to minimize the cost function by moving downhill.
g.text: An iterative optimization algorithm that finds the minimum of the cost function.

g.data-narration: Multiple Linear Regression extends simple regression to use multiple features, creating a predictive model with multiple weights.
g.text: Real-world problems use multiple features. Each feature gets its own weight coefficient.
y = β₀ + β₁x₁ + β₂x₂ + ... + βpxp + ε

g.data-narration: We evaluate regression models using MAE, RMSE, and R² to understand prediction accuracy and how much variance the model explains.
Regression finds the best-fit line that minimizes prediction error, using MSE as the cost function optimized by Gradient Descent.

g.text: Simple: one feature (y = β₀ + β₁x), Multiple: many features

g.data-narration: Regression powers real-world applications from price prediction to risk analysis, forming the foundation for advanced machine learning.
